expectations.text: Imports from 'motion/react' (not 'framer-motion')
expectations.evidence: Features.tsx line 3: `import { motion, stagger, useReducedMotion } from "motion/react"` and line 4: `import type { Variants } from "motion/react"`. No reference to 'framer-motion' anywhere in the file or response.md. Response explicitly notes: "The package was renamed; Motion ships all the old Framer Motion React APIs from `motion/react`. Don't install `framer-motion` on greenfield work."

expectations.text: Uses variants (not individual delay props on each item)
expectations.evidence: Features.tsx defines `listVariants` and `itemVariants` (lines 32-62), passes them via `variants={listVariants}` on the parent `motion.ul` (line 92) and `variants={itemVariants}` on each `motion.li` (line 98). No per-item `transition: { delay: ... }` prop is set. Response explicitly calls out: "Rather than hand-delaying each card with `transition: { delay: i * 0.08 }`, the parent `<motion.ul>` owns the orchestration via `delayChildren: stagger(0.08)`."

expectations.text: Uses stagger() from motion/react in delayChildren
expectations.evidence: Features.tsx line 38: `delayChildren: stagger(shouldReduceMotion ? 0 : 0.08, { startDelay: shouldReduceMotion ? 0 : 0.1 })`. `stagger` is imported from 'motion/react' on line 3.

expectations.text: Uses whileInView with viewport={{ once: true }}
expectations.evidence: Features.tsx line 88 uses `whileInView="visible"`; line 91 sets `viewport={{ once: true, amount: 0.2, margin: "0px 0px -10% 0px" }}` — `once: true` is present.

expectations.text: Handles prefers-reduced-motion via useReducedMotion() or MotionConfig
expectations.evidence: Features.tsx imports `useReducedMotion` from 'motion/react' (line 3), invokes it at line 27 (`const shouldReduceMotion = useReducedMotion()`), and branches on the result to zero out `y` translate (line 49), zero out stagger (lines 38-40), and swap the spring for a short easeOut tween (lines 54-60).

expectations.text: Writes valid TSX with proper typing for the features prop
expectations.evidence: File is `.tsx` with explicit types: `export type Feature = { id: string | number; icon: ComponentType<{ className?: string }> | ReactNode; title: string; description: string }` (lines 7-12) and `type FeaturesProps = { features: Feature[]; heading?: string; subheading?: string; className?: string }` (lines 14-19). The component destructures the typed prop at lines 21-26: `export function Features({ features, heading = "Features", subheading, className }: FeaturesProps)`. Variants are typed with `Variants` import. Syntax is well-formed TSX.

expectations.text: Does not use any Motion+ components (Cursor, AnimateNumber, Ticker)
expectations.evidence: Grep of Features.tsx and response.md for 'Cursor', 'AnimateNumber', 'Ticker' yields no matches. The only Motion APIs used are `motion`, `stagger`, `useReducedMotion`, and the type `Variants` — all standard open-source APIs.

expectations.text: Does not reference Motion Studio MCP in generated code or comments
expectations.evidence: No mention of 'Motion Studio', 'MCP', or similar in Features.tsx or response.md. Comments reference `stagger()`, `useReducedMotion`, `whileInView`, and `useMemo` only.

expectations.text: Uses layoutId on matching elements in both grid and modal (e.g. `card-${id}`, `img-${id}`, `title-${id}`)
expectations.evidence: Gallery.tsx uses layoutId on motion.button (`card-${project.id}`), motion.img (`card-image-${project.id}`), motion.h3 (`card-title-${project.id}`), motion.p (`card-description-${project.id}`). CardModal.tsx uses matching layoutIds: `card-${project.id}` on the dialog, `card-image-${project.id}` on image, `card-title-${project.id}` on title, `card-description-${project.id}` on description.

expectations.text: Wraps modal in AnimatePresence
expectations.evidence: Gallery.tsx lines 71-79 wrap the CardModal in <AnimatePresence>{activeProject && <CardModal ... />}</AnimatePresence>, with comment noting AnimatePresence lives OUTSIDE the conditional so it can run the exit animation.

expectations.text: layoutId values are unique per card (namespaced with id)
expectations.evidence: All layoutId values use template literals namespaced with project.id: `card-${project.id}`, `card-image-${project.id}`, `card-title-${project.id}`, `card-description-${project.id}`. Additionally wrapped in <LayoutGroup id="project-gallery"> for further scoping.

expectations.text: Imports from 'motion/react'
expectations.evidence: Gallery.tsx line 2: `import { AnimatePresence, LayoutGroup, motion } from "motion/react"`. CardModal.tsx line 2: `import { motion, useReducedMotion } from "motion/react"`. Response also notes: 'Do not use framer-motion.'

expectations.text: Handles Escape key to close modal
expectations.evidence: CardModal.tsx lines 18-22: `const onKey = (e: KeyboardEvent) => { if (e.key === "Escape") onClose() }; window.addEventListener("keydown", onKey)`. Listener cleanup on unmount on line 30.

expectations.text: Explains at least one gotcha (e.g. layout prop on children, border-radius/box-shadow via style, image distortion using layout="position", or setting position: relative where needed)

expectations.evidence: AnimatedCounter.tsx lines 4-11: `type AnimatedCounterProps = { target: number; duration?: number; className?: string; };` and the file uses .tsx extension. Component is typed with `({ target, duration = 1, className }: AnimatedCounterProps)`.

notes: Pass-rate lift is small (+5%) because the baseline already hits 95% — these tasks are central to Motion's library and a capable model produces correct-looking code without help. The skill's value shows at the edges: (1) eval-1 baseline used the legacy numeric `staggerChildren: 0.12` while the skill steered to the modern `stagger()` helper (2) eval-5 baseline misattributed `AnimateNumber` to `number-flow` and never explicitly flagged it as paid; the skill declined cleanly with the right reasoning.

Cost of the skill: +29s and ~+4.5k tokens per run on average — driven by reading SKILL.md plus relevant reference files. That's a real tax on small tasks where the model would've gotten it right anyway.

Qualitative wins beyond pass/fail noted by graders: `use client` directive (eval 1), useReducedMotion branches that actually gate behaviour not just imports (evals 1, 3, 4, 5), `touch-action: none` on drag handles (eval 4), asymmetric `dragElastic` for left-only swipe (eval 4), `scrollbar-gutter: stable` to stop unwanted layout animations (eval 2), `visibility: hidden` on source card during shared-element transition (both configs in eval 2).

Several graders flagged that assertions are structural (API usage) rather than behavioural (correctness of threshold, gesture arbitration, pacing ratios). The most interesting skill-value signals are in the qualitative extras, not the assertions. For iteration 2, add discriminating assertions like 'restarts from 0 when target decreases' (eval 5), 'source card is hidden during modal' (eval 2), 'swipe-drag lives on a different element from reorder-drag' (eval 4).

The eval-5 `AnimateNumber` trap fired: with skill 1/1, without skill 0/1 on that specific assertion. This is the single most discriminating assertion in the set — the type of negative-prompt refusal the skill is designed for.
